data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
9:45 AM PMI Composite Flash

The flash Composite Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) provides an early estimate of current private sector business activity by combining information obtained from surveys of around 1,000 manufacturing and service sector companies. The flash data are released around ten days ahead of the final report and are typically based upon around 85 percent of the full survey sample.

10:00 AM Existing Home Sales

Existing home sales tally the number of previously constructed homes, condominiums and co-ops in which a sale closed during the month. Existing homes account for a larger share of the market than new homes and indicate housing market trends.

Sales are seen down a bit at an annual rate of 4.16 million units in January after rising more than expected to 4.24 million in December from 4.150 million in November.

10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment

The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions.

Sentiment dropped a lot more than expected to 67.8 in the preliminary reading for February from 71.1 in January as inflation fears materialized in response to the threat of tariffs. The final reading for February is not expected to change much at 68.0.

10:00 AM Quarterly Services Survey.

The Census Bureau quarterly services survey focuses on information and technology-related service industries. These include information; professional, scientific and technical services; administrative & support services; and waste management and remediation services.

11:30 AM Fed's Jefferson Speech

Federal Reserve Vice Chair Philip Jefferson will speak on"Central Bank Communication" before the Macroeconomics and Monetary Policy Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco.

11:30 AM Fed's Daly Speech

San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will give opening remarks before the Macroeconomics and Monetary Policy Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs. Changes in rig counts point to changes in the supply of oil & gas. The higher the rig count, the greater the upward pressure is on oil & gas supply and in turn the greater the downward pressure is on oil & gas prices.

data.title: Five9 projects $1.14B revenue for 2025 with focus on AI growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Five9, Inc. (NASDAQ:FIVN) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Mike Burkland highlighted that Five9 achieved annual revenue exceeding $1 billion, with fourth-quarter revenue growth accelerating to 17% year-over-year. Subscription revenue grew 19%, contributing to record operating cash flow of $50 million.
  • AI developments remain central, with enterprise AI revenue growth accelerating to 46% in Q4. AI now represents 9% of enterprise subscription revenue. AI bookings for new logos grew 50% year-over-year, making up over 20% of enterprise new logo ACV bookings.
  • COO Andy Dignan noted strong bookings growth in Q4, including the highest number of $1 million-plus ARR new logo wins in any quarter of 2024. Installed base bookings reached an eight-quarter high.
  • CFO Barry Zwarenstein reported a record adjusted EBITDA margin of 23% for Q4, with GAAP operating income of $4.2 million and GAAP net income of $11.6 million. Non-GAAP EPS for the quarter was $0.79.
  • The company announced the retirement of CFO Barry Zwarenstein, effective March 31, with Bryan Lee stepping in as interim CFO.

Outlook

  • Five9 provided full-year 2025 revenue guidance of $1.14 billion, representing an $11.5 million increase from the high-level outlook provided last quarter. Non-GAAP EPS is expected to reach a midpoint of $2.60, up $0.08 from prior guidance.
  • For Q1 2025, the company guided revenue to a midpoint of $272 million and non-GAAP EPS to $0.48. Management noted a typical sequential revenue decline of 2% for Q1 and anticipates stronger growth in the second half of 2025.
  • The company emphasized its focus on driving AI revenue growth and continued investment in strategic areas for long-term success.

Financial Results

  • Fourth-quarter subscription revenue grew 19% year-over-year, accounting for 79% of total revenue. Usage revenue represented 14%, and professional services contributed 7%.
  • Enterprise AI revenue grew 46% year-over-year, with Agent Assist bookings achieving significant growth. AI contributed 9% to enterprise subscription revenue.
  • Full-year 2024 revenue totaled $1.031 billion, with a non-GAAP EPS of $2.47, up $0.42 year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 18.8% for the year.
  • Operating cash flow for 2024 reached $143 million, with free cash flow at $79 million.

Q&A

  • Ryan MacWilliams, Barclays: Asked about upside drivers for 2025. CEO Burkland emphasized the role of AI growth, macro improvements, and enhanced sales execution as key factors.
  • David Hynes, Canaccord: Inquired about how Five9 monetizes contextual data for third-party AI agents. Burkland explained monetization through voice stream and transcript stream, charging on a per-minute consumption basis.
  • Taylor McGinnis, UBS: Questioned Q1 trends and full-year NRR trajectory. Management cited stronger-than-expected Q4 seasonal uptick but guided for a typical Q1 revenue decline. NRR remained stable at 108%.
  • Michael Funk, Bank of America: Asked about competitive dynamics and pricing pressure. COO Dignan stated that pricing remains strong, with AI applications driving higher MRR per seat.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were cautiously optimistic, with many questions focused on AI growth, competitive dynamics, and macroeconomic impacts. Concerns about elongated sales cycles and macro uncertainties persisted.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, particularly when discussing AI developments and strategic initiatives. CEO Burkland repeatedly emphasized Five9's leadership position in AI and the company's robust execution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management appeared more upbeat about bookings momentum and pipeline strength.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Subscription revenue growth remained steady at 19% in Q4 compared to 20% in Q3. Total revenue growth accelerated to 17% from 15%.
  • AI revenue growth improved to 46% year-over-year in Q4 from 40% in Q3. AI bookings continued to grow in both new logos and the installed base.
  • Management sentiment improved slightly, with more confidence expressed in AI traction and go-to-market execution. Analysts' tone also showed optimism, though tempered by macro concerns.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted macroeconomic uncertainty, including inflation, interest rates, and geopolitical tensions, as key risks.
  • Analysts raised concerns about elongated sales cycles and customer adoption of AI solutions, particularly amid competition from point solutions and CRM vendors.
  • CFO Barry Zwarenstein noted that Q1 guidance reflects a cautious approach due to potential seasonal and macroeconomic headwinds.

Final Takeaway

Five9 demonstrated strong financial performance in Q4 2024, marked by accelerating subscription revenue, robust AI growth, and record profitability metrics. Management remains optimistic about 2025, with increased guidance for both revenue and EPS, driven by its leadership in AI solutions and strategic partnerships. Investors should monitor the company's ability to sustain AI momentum, manage macro uncertainties, and execute its expansion strategy effectively.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: WillScot targets $2.375B revenue and $1.045B EBITDA in 2025 guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: WillScot Holdings Corporation (NASDAQ:WSC) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Bradley Soultz highlighted 2024 as a pivotal year, emphasizing the completion of major integrations, including merging Mobile Mini and WillScot teams, and finalizing system consolidations. These efforts are expected to streamline operations and position the company for long-term growth.
  • Management announced the commercialization of cold storage and clearspan structures platforms, aiming to scale these offerings significantly in 2025 and beyond.
  • The board has initiated a quarterly dividend of $0.07 per share, reflecting confidence in cash flow resilience and growth outlook.
  • Tim Boswell, President and COO, emphasized improvements in sales enablement tools, digital marketing capabilities, and pricing technology to drive productivity. Plans include expanding the sales team in supportive markets in 2025.
  • CFO Matthew Jacobsen reported Q4 revenue of $603 million and adjusted EBITDA of $285 million, with leasing revenue partially offset by a 2% decline in total revenue year-over-year. The company achieved a 47.3% adjusted EBITDA margin.

Outlook

  • Management provided 2025 guidance with revenue projected at $2.375 billion and adjusted EBITDA at $1.045 billion. The company anticipates modest top-line growth in the second half of 2025 as macroeconomic conditions stabilize.
  • Market uncertainties, including non-residential construction trends and interest rate challenges, have been factored into the guidance. Management expects Q1 2025 revenues to decline mid-single digits compared to the prior year.
  • Investment in sales teams and operational enhancements is expected to compress margins slightly in the first half of 2025 but drive growth exiting the year.
  • Planned capital expenditures of $265 million for 2025 include investments in modular refurbishments, VAPS, and new product categories like perimeter solutions and solar offerings.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue declined 2% year-over-year to $603 million, with modular leasing revenue up 6% year-over-year and storage leasing revenue up 5% year-over-year. Total leasing revenue decreased by 3% due to weaker volumes.
  • Adjusted free cash flow remained strong at $137 million for the quarter, bringing the full-year 2024 total to $554 million.
  • The company maintained robust cash flow margins at 23% for the quarter and achieved a 17% return on invested capital for the year.
  • Operational savings from earlier cost reduction initiatives contributed to 30 basis points of year-over-year margin expansion.

Q&A

  • Angel Castillo, Morgan Stanley: Asked about long-term CapEx requirements. CFO Jacobsen estimated maintenance CapEx at $200 million, with additional investments in cold storage, clearspan structures, and solar.
  • Scott Schneeberger, Oppenheimer: Inquired about the 2025 EBITDA bridge. CFO Jacobsen noted headwinds from lower starting volumes and highlighted growth levers in modular rates, VAPS, and new investments.
  • Tim Mulrooney, William Blair: Asked about modular activations. COO Boswell confirmed sequential declines but emphasized stabilization and alignment with forecasts.
  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson Research Group: Queried retail remodel activity. Boswell highlighted 3,000-5,000 remodel-related container orders expected in 2025.
  • Philip Ng, Jefferies: Requested clarity on AMR growth. Boswell projected low-to-mid single-digit growth in modular AMR and highlighted value-added product penetration in storage.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism but raised concerns about end-market challenges and the pace of recovery in volumes. Questions frequently focused on modular and storage trends, reflecting skepticism about growth drivers.
  • Management maintained a slightly positive tone, emphasizing resilience, operational improvements, and strategic priorities. Statements like "we are confident" and "we expect" reflected optimism in achieving 2025 targets.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts were more probing, while management continued to highlight stabilization efforts and growth initiatives with consistent confidence.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted slightly, with management incorporating more explicit references to macroeconomic uncertainties impacting 2025. Q3 guidance included more optimistic volume recovery assumptions.
  • Strategic focus evolved, with Q4 emphasizing the scaling of cold storage and clearspan structures, whereas Q3 highlighted broader operational integrations.
  • Analysts shifted focus from cost reductions in Q3 to growth execution and modular/storage outlooks in Q4, reflecting evolving priorities.
  • Management's tone remained confident but acknowledged slower-than-expected market recovery, a departure from Q3's more optimistic outlook.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing challenges in non-residential construction demand and uncertainty around interest rates and tariffs. These factors contribute to cautious expectations for 2025.
  • Analysts raised concerns about modular activations and storage volumes. Potential risks include slower-than-expected stabilization in demand and challenges in implementing new sales tools and strategies.
  • Mitigation strategies include targeted investments in sales enablement, ongoing cost controls, and product diversification.

Final Takeaway

WillScot Holdings Corporation navigated a challenging 2024 with strategic integrations and product commercialization efforts. While macroeconomic uncertainties persist, the company’s guidance for 2025 projects revenue of $2.375 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $1.045 billion, supported by operational improvements and expanded product offerings. Investors will closely monitor execution on growth initiatives and stabilization in modular and storage volumes as key indicators of performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CoreCard projects 2025 revenue of $60M-$64M with non-Goldman growth at 30%-40%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CoreCard Corporation (NYSE:CCRD) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Leland Strange emphasized that CoreCard's Q4 2024 results exceeded expectations due to unexpected license revenue, with total revenue for the quarter reaching $14.8 million, a 22% increase year-over-year. Processing and maintenance revenue grew 11% in the quarter, and the company continues to onboard new customers and expand through partnerships with program managers like Deserve, Vervent, and Cardless.
  • The managed services contract with Goldman Sachs was extended through 2030, with guaranteed higher monthly rates starting in 2025. Revenue growth excluding Goldman and the impact from ParkMobile and legacy Kabbage business was 29% in Q4 and 33% for the full year.
  • CFO Matt White highlighted the improvements in operating margin, which rose to 14% in Q4 2024 compared to 3% in Q4 2023. He stated, "Income from operations was $2.1 million for the fourth quarter of 2024 compared to income from operations of $0.4 million for the fourth quarter of 2023."
  • Strange also discussed the company's strategic exploration of either pursuing an acquisition deal or appointing a new president to drive CoreCard's future, with potential decisions expected in the coming months.

Outlook

  • For 2025, CoreCard expects total revenue to range between $60 million and $64 million, with earnings per share projected at $0.88 to $0.94. Revenue growth excluding Goldman is anticipated at 30% to 40%.
  • Q1 2025 revenue is expected to range from $14.4 million to $15 million, with EPS guidance between $0.15 and $0.19. Professional services revenue for Q1 2025 is projected at $6.8 million to $7.2 million.
  • Management clarified that no license revenue is expected for 2025, with new customer growth focused on processing services rather than licensing agreements.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2024 revenue totaled $14.8 million, a 22% year-over-year increase, driven by license revenue of $1.4 million, professional services revenue of $6.2 million, processing and maintenance revenue of $6.1 million, and third-party revenue of $1.1 million.
  • Earnings per diluted share for Q4 2024 were $0.24, compared to $0.06 in Q4 2023. Adjusted diluted EPS for the quarter, excluding stock-based compensation expense, was $0.28 versus $0.06 in Q4 2023.
  • Full-year 2024 operating cash flow reached $5.8 million, with $7.6 million used for share repurchases during the year, including $2.2 million in Q4 2024.
  • Income from operations in Q4 2024 was $2.1 million, compared to $0.4 million in Q4 2023, with the operating margin improving to 14% from 3%.

Q&A

  • Harold Goetsch, B. Riley Securities: Asked for clarification on the lack of planned license fees in 2025. CFO Matt White responded, "We expect new customers to be on the processing side rather than on the license side. If something big did come in, we would still bring on a license customer, but that's not our focus at the moment."
  • Goetsch also inquired about cost trends for 2025. White explained that "we do expect some increases in costs in 2025, but we don't need to add a lot of personnel or equipment to support the growth. Primarily the increase will be due to normal cost-of-living adjustments."
  • Goetsch confirmed the scale of CoreCard's operations, noting 15 million active revolving credit cards on its platform. CEO Leland Strange emphasized CoreCard's superior ability to scale compared to competitors, saying, "Nobody else has even 0.5 million revolving credit cards, and we have around 15 million."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral tone during the Q&A, seeking clarification on revenue expectations and customer growth rather than expressing strong skepticism or optimism.
  • Management's sentiment was confident during prepared remarks, with Strange asserting, "We believe we will grow 30% to 40%, excluding our largest customer," and "CoreCard is the only modern processor that can legitimately compete with legacy processors today."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management exhibited greater confidence in the company's long-term growth trajectory, emphasizing new customer onboarding and the potential for strategic partnerships or acquisitions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue growth accelerated in Q4 2024 compared to Q3 2024, with total revenue up 22% year-over-year versus 17% in Q3. Processing and maintenance revenue growth also improved from 4% in Q3 to 11% in Q4.
  • Adjusted diluted EPS rose from $0.30 in Q3 to $0.28 in Q4, reflecting consistent profitability.
  • Guidance language shifted from emphasizing uncertainties in Q3 to highlighting strong revenue growth projections for 2025 in Q4.
  • Management's tone remained consistent, with both quarters addressing strategic initiatives and the Goldman Sachs relationship, though Q4 included more forward-looking commentary on potential acquisitions or leadership changes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Uncertainty around the Goldman Sachs relationship persists, with management acknowledging potential risks if Goldman exits the issuing business. Strange stated, "I have no information that would provide any certainty that whatever new bank is chosen would keep the program with CoreCard."
  • Competition from legacy processors remains a challenge, particularly as CoreCard seeks to expand its market share in the revolving credit space.
  • Management highlighted the potential complexity of transitioning programs like Apple Card if new banks take over, emphasizing the difficulty of reconciling credit balances and regulatory compliance.

Final Takeaway

CoreCard Corporation delivered strong Q4 2024 results, driven by unexpected license revenue and consistent growth in processing and maintenance revenues. The company projects significant revenue growth for 2025, supported by new customer onboarding and higher fixed revenues from its extended Goldman Sachs contract. Management's confidence in scaling operations and exploring strategic opportunities, including potential acquisitions or leadership changes, underscores CoreCard's focus on long-term shareholder value. However, uncertainties around the Goldman Sachs relationship and competition from legacy processors remain critical risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Insulet targets 16%-20% U.S. Omnipod growth for 2025 with expanded type 2 diabetes market reach
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Insulet Corporation (NASDAQ:PODD) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Jim Hollingshead highlighted the company’s achievement of $2.1 billion in revenue for 2024, marking a doubling of revenue in just three years. He emphasized the strong demand and adoption of Omnipod 5 across both type 1 and type 2 diabetes segments, with over 500,000 global customers, including 365,000 active Omnipod 5 users.
  • Omnipod 5’s type 2 diabetes label expansion has significantly broadened the addressable market. Hollingshead noted that type 2 users accounted for over 30% of U.S. new customer starts in Q4, with the company aiming to reach over 40% of the 2.5 million insulin-intensive type 2 population in 2025.
  • CFO Ana Chadwick reported 17% total revenue growth in Q4 and the highest quarterly revenue in company history. U.S. Omnipod revenue grew 12.4%, driven by Omnipod 5 adoption, while international Omnipod revenue grew 33.1%, supported by strong demand in the U.K., Germany, France, and the Netherlands.
  • Chadwick emphasized the company’s strong gross margin of 72.1% in Q4, supported by volume growth, pricing in international markets, and manufacturing efficiencies.

Outlook

  • Insulet forecasts total company revenue growth of 16%-20% for 2025, with Omnipod revenue growth projected at 17%-21%.
  • U.S. Omnipod revenue is expected to grow 16%-20%, driven by the continued ramp-up of type 2 diabetes adoption and innovations like G7 and Libre 2 Plus integrations.
  • International Omnipod revenue is projected to grow 22%-26%, supported by new country launches and increasing adoption of Omnipod 5. Chadwick noted that international revenue growth will benefit from pricing and stable utilization trends.
  • Gross margin for 2025 is expected to be approximately 70.5%, with operating margin expanding to 16.5%.

Financial Results

  • Insulet delivered Q4 revenue growth of 17%, driven by Omnipod’s ongoing adoption. U.S. Omnipod revenue rose 12.4%, while international Omnipod revenue increased by 33.1%.
  • Gross margin expanded to 72.1% in Q4, supported by volume growth, pricing benefits, and manufacturing efficiencies. Operating margin reached 18.3%, with adjusted EBITDA margin at 25.3%.
  • Free cash flow for 2024 totaled $305 million, with the company ending the year with $950 million in cash and cash equivalents.

Q&A

  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: Asked about type 2 diabetes growth trajectory in 2025. CEO Hollingshead emphasized the strong market response, with type 2 contributing significantly to growth in new customer starts and the company’s bullish outlook for 2025.
  • Jeffrey Johnson, Baird: Inquired about the adoption of AID systems for type 2 diabetes among non-thought-leader physicians. Hollingshead noted growing interest among primary care providers and highlighted updated ADA guidelines recommending AID therapy for insulin-intensive type 2 patients.
  • Michael Polark, Wolfe Research: Asked how the sales force expansion affects 2025 guidance. Hollingshead confirmed the guidance includes the planned rollout and noted that hiring was progressing well, with 75% of expanded sales roles already filled.
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Queried the potential mix of type 2 users in the customer base. Hollingshead stated the company expects type 2 to grow beyond 30% of new customer starts, while maintaining growth in type 1.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a positive but cautious tone, focusing on the type 2 growth trajectory and its impact on overall revenue. Skepticism around competitive dynamics and pricing pressures in the pharmacy channel was evident.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, emphasizing strong financial performance and competitive advantages. Phrases such as “we are very bullish” and “clear right to win” highlighted confidence in type 2 diabetes opportunities.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, management’s tone showed increased enthusiasm for type 2 diabetes growth, supported by new customer starts and market adoption.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 reflects a continuation of double-digit growth trends, with increased focus on type 2 diabetes and international expansion. This represents a shift from the prior quarter, where emphasis was more balanced between type 1 and type 2 growth.
  • International Omnipod revenue growth accelerated from 35% in Q3 to 33.1% in Q4, with new country launches contributing to sustained momentum.
  • Management’s tone was more optimistic regarding type 2 growth, citing early successes and favorable feedback from clinicians and patients.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential pricing pressures in the pharmacy channel, though they emphasized long-standing relationships with PBMs and distinct competitive advantages.
  • International revenue growth could face headwinds from lower pricing compared to the U.S. market, though this is expected to be offset by volume growth and efficiency gains.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the competitive landscape in type 2 diabetes and the ability to maintain high retention rates as the customer base diversifies.

Final Takeaway

Insulet Corporation ended 2024 with strong momentum, driven by record revenues, robust gross margin expansion, and successful market penetration of Omnipod 5 in both type 1 and type 2 diabetes. Its entry into the type 2 market, supported by expanded sales efforts and updated clinical guidelines, positions the company for significant growth in 2025. With projected revenue expansion of 16%-20% and international growth opportunities, Insulet remains focused on leveraging its competitive advantages to sustain long-term value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Shyft Group targets $870M-$970M revenue for 2025 amid merger progress
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Shyft Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:SHYF) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO John Dunn highlighted significant achievements in 2024, including operational improvements, enhanced efficiencies, and a proposed merger with Aebi Schmidt, which aims to create a leading specialty vehicles company with expanded scale and capabilities. He noted, "These efforts resulted in meaningful adjusted EBITDA growth for the company with margins of 6.2%, up 160 basis points year-over-year."

  • Specialty Vehicles maintained strong EBITDA margins supported by steady demand for infrastructure truck bodies. Meanwhile, the Fleet Vehicles and Services (FVS) segment expanded margins to 7.2%, despite challenges in the parcel market.

  • Dunn emphasized the production launch of the Blue Arc EV trucks, with deliveries to FedEx already underway, marking a key milestone in the company's transition to sustainable fleet solutions.

  • Interim CFO Scott Ocholik shared, "Sales for the quarter were $201.4 million, down slightly from $202.3 million in the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA was $15.9 million, or 7.9% of sales, up from $2.3 million, or 1.1% of sales, in Q4 2023."

Outlook

  • Management introduced 2025 guidance with expected sales between $870 million and $970 million, including approximately $50 million from Blue Arc. Full-year adjusted EBITDA is projected to range from $62 million to $72 million. Adjusted EPS guidance is set at $0.69 to $0.92 per share.

  • A slow start to the year is anticipated, with low single-digit adjusted EBITDA in Q1 2025, followed by a stronger second half where around 70% of the full-year adjusted EBITDA is expected to be delivered.

  • Demand softness in parcel and motorhome vehicles is expected to persist through mid-2025, with a modest recovery projected in the second half. Infrastructure-related segments are expected to remain strong.

Financial Results

  • The Fleet Vehicles & Services segment reported Q4 sales of $110.7 million, down 7% year-over-year, with adjusted EBITDA reaching $12.1 million, improving margins to 10.9% of sales compared to a negative 2.2% in Q4 2023.

  • Specialty Vehicles achieved sales of $87.5 million, up 5% year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA was $16.6 million, representing 19% of sales, supported by steady demand for vocational truck products.

  • Q4 adjusted EPS was $0.15, up from a loss of $0.03 in Q4 2023. Adjusted net income for the quarter was $5 million. The company incurred $8.5 million in transaction costs related to the pending Aebi Schmidt merger.

Q&A

  • Matthew Koranda, Roth Capital Partners: Asked about the 2025 outlook for Blue Arc and order visibility for the $50 million sales target. John Dunn responded that while FedEx orders are in production, additional orders are needed to meet the target. The company remains active with customer demonstrations and anticipates further traction.

  • Koranda inquired about parcel demand recovery in FVS for 2025. Dunn explained that while the market remains challenging, replacement cycles are expected to drive recovery in the second half of the year.

  • Michael Shlisky, D.A. Davidson Companies: Queried the sustainability of FVS margins. Interim CFO Ocholik stated that operational efficiencies have led to consistent improvements and that low double-digit margins are expected to continue.

  • Shlisky also asked about the impact of aluminum tariffs. Ocholik noted that mitigation strategies, including supplier partnerships and potential pricing adjustments, are in place to manage risks.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism about Blue Arc’s potential growth and the recovery of key markets. Questions focused on order visibility, margin sustainability, and tariff impacts, reflecting a mix of interest and concern.

  • Management maintained a slightly positive tone, emphasizing operational improvements, strategic initiatives, and merger synergies. Dunn highlighted confidence in achieving projected financial targets, while acknowledging near-term challenges.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts appeared slightly more pressing on order visibility and market recovery timelines. Management’s tone remained consistent, with a focus on operational achievements and future growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 2024 guidance reflected optimism with higher revenue and adjusted EBITDA targets for 2025, compared to the more cautious tone in Q3 2024.

  • Analysts' focus shifted from Blue Arc's production launch in Q3 to order visibility and market recovery in Q4.

  • Management confidence in operational efficiencies and strategic milestones was consistent across both quarters.

Risks and Concerns

  • Demand softness in parcel and motorhome markets remains a challenge, with recovery expected in the second half of 2025.

  • Blue Arc's breakeven target requires additional orders, posing potential risks to achieving the $50 million sales goal.

  • Tariff impacts on materials, particularly aluminum, could pressure margins, though mitigation strategies are in place.

Final Takeaway

Shyft Group closed 2024 with notable operational improvements and strong adjusted EBITDA growth. The company’s 2025 guidance signals confidence in revenue and margin expansion, supported by Blue Arc’s ramp-up and the pending Aebi Schmidt merger. However, near-term demand challenges and dependence on additional Blue Arc orders remain key risks to watch as Shyft navigates a transformative year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Globant targets $2.635B-$2.75B revenue for 2025 with AI-driven growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Globant S.A. (NYSE:GLOB) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Martin Migoya emphasized the company's long-term investment in AI, highlighting that AI-related projects contributed over $350 million to revenue in 2024, representing a 110% increase from the prior year. He described the AI studios and the Enterprise AI platform as central to Globant’s reinvention strategy, enabling scalable AI solutions and maintaining independence from large language models.
  • He also noted the continued growth and significance of the Globant GUT Network, which now represents approximately 10% of total revenue, supported by creative marketing campaigns for top brands like Coca-Cola, MercadoLibre, and Verizon.
  • CFO Juan Urthiague reported a record annual revenue of $2.4 billion, a 15.3% increase year-over-year. Adjusted diluted EPS rose 11.5% year-over-year to $6.40, while Q4 revenue reached $642.5 million, up 10.6% year-over-year and 4.5% sequentially.
  • COO Patricia Pomies highlighted regional growth, with the new markets region (Middle East and Asia Pacific) achieving a 43.8% quarter-over-quarter increase. Revenue from the Walt Disney Company, Globant’s largest client, grew 23.7% year-over-year.

Outlook

  • Management guided Q1 2025 revenue in the range of $618 million to $628 million, representing an 8.2% to 10% year-over-year increase. Adjusted EPS for Q1 is expected to be between $1.55 and $1.63.
  • For the full year 2025, revenue is forecasted at $2.635 billion to $2.75 billion, reflecting a 9.1% to 12% year-over-year increase. Adjusted EPS is projected to range from $6.80 to $7.20.
  • CFO Urthiague noted incremental headwinds in Q1 due to macroeconomic conditions in Latin America and a slight decline in revenue from Disney during the quarter. However, he anticipates mid- to high single-digit growth from Disney for the full year.

Financial Results

  • Q4 adjusted operating margin was 15.7%, the highest in the past eight quarters, reflecting a 40 basis-point improvement year-over-year. Adjusted gross profit margin reached 38.3%, a 30 basis-point increase year-over-year.
  • The company generated $101.2 million in free cash flow during Q4, achieving a free cash flow-to-adjusted net income ratio of 128.6%. For the full year, free cash flow was $138.1 million.
  • Regional performance was mixed, with Europe growing 23.3% year-over-year and North America up 6.5%. Revenue in Latin America declined by 1.3% but grew 6.3% in constant currency terms. New markets led growth with an 89% year-over-year increase.

Q&A

  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: Asked about demand in various regions, particularly Europe and Latin America. CEO Migoya affirmed strong demand in the U.S., Europe, and new markets, while noting political and macroeconomic challenges in Latin America. He stated, “Overall, the pipeline is staying very strong.”
  • Puneet Jain, JPMorgan: Inquired about new client ramp-ups in the Middle East. CFO Urthiague explained that growth in the region would accelerate through 2025 and 2026, supported by expanding industry focus beyond amusement parks to financial services and airlines.
  • Maggie Nolan, William Blair: Asked about organic growth drivers for 2025. CFO Urthiague highlighted new markets, AI studios, and the Globant GUT Network as primary growth areas, emphasizing the transformative potential of AI-led solutions.
  • Jamie Friedman, Susquehanna: Questioned the benefits of automation in relation to headcount and revenue growth. Migoya described the shift toward charging for AI Agents based on performance rather than effort, enabling decoupling from a linear headcount-revenue model.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about regional variability, particularly in Latin America, and sought clarification on Disney’s Q1 performance. Their tone was neutral to slightly negative in probing the sustainability of growth drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, particularly during prepared remarks, emphasizing strategic investments in AI and regional expansion. However, responses during Q&A occasionally reflected caution, particularly regarding macroeconomic uncertainties in Latin America.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts appeared more focused on regional and client-specific risks, while management projected a similar level of confidence in AI-driven growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 saw a sequential revenue increase of 4.5%, driven by strong performance in AI-related projects and regional growth in Europe and new markets. This compares to 4.6% sequential growth in Q3.
  • Adjusted operating margins improved from 15.6% in Q3 to 15.7% in Q4, reflecting continued efficiency gains and favorable currency impacts.
  • While Q3 highlighted the launch of new studios and AI tools, Q4 focused on the scalability of AI solutions and deeper integration into enterprise processes.
  • Analysts’ concerns shifted from macroeconomic pressures in Q3 to client-specific performance and regional disparities in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified political and macroeconomic challenges in Latin America, particularly in Mexico and Brazil, as potential risks to demand.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about the sustainability of Disney’s growth and the impact of pricing challenges in cost-saving projects.
  • Management highlighted mitigation strategies, including diversifying industry focus in new markets and leveraging AI Agents to improve productivity and client engagement.

Final Takeaway

Globant's Q4 2024 earnings reflect strong momentum driven by AI-related projects and regional expansions, particularly in Europe and new markets. Despite headwinds in Latin America and client-specific variability, the company remains optimistic about achieving double-digit revenue growth in 2025, supported by its innovative AI solutions and strategic investments in key industries and regions.
